title: Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah
tagline: Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah — spiritual town of Bihar

overview.short: Aranya Devi Temple is a Hindu temple located in Arrah of Bihar, India.
overview.description: Aranya Devi Temple is a Hindu temple located in Arrah of Bihar, India. Ara, the headquarters of Bhojpur district is named after the goddess of this temple. The Hindu goddess worshipped here is considered to be the presiding deity of the city and the deity of the people there. According to Devi Bhagavata Purana, this temple, recognized from Satya Yuga to Kali Yuga, is also a Siddh Pitha along with 108 Shakti Pithas.

topPlaces.name: Parashanatha temple, Arrah
topPlaces.description: Parashanatha Temple is a Jain temple dedicated to Parasnath in Masarh village near Arrah in Bihar.

topPlaces.name: Ramna Maidan
topPlaces.description: Babu Veer Kunwar Singh Ramna Maidan is located in Arrah, Bhojpur District, Bihar. It is one of the largest urban parks in Arrah and is spread across 60 acres of land.

topPlaces.name: Veer Kunwar Singh Stadium
topPlaces.description: Veer Kunwar Singh Stadium is a multi-purpose stadium located in Arrah, Bihar. The stadium has capacity of 10,000 spectators.

topPlaces.name: Siege of Arrah
topPlaces.description: The siege of Arrah (27 July – 3 August 1857) took place during the Indian Mutiny (also known as the Indian Rebellion of 1857). It was the eight-day defence of a fortified outbuilding, occupied by a combination of 18 civilians and 50 members of the Bengal Military Police Battalion, against 2,500 to 3,000 mutinying Bengal Native Infantry sepoys from three regiments and an estimated 8,000 men from irregular forces commanded by Kunwar Singh, the local zamindar or chieftain who controlled the Jagdishpur estate.

topPlaces.name: Arrah
topPlaces.description: Arrah (also spelled as Arra, Arah, transliterated as Ara) is a city, a municipal corporation and the administrative headquarters of Bhojpur district (formerly known as Shahabad district) in the Indian state of Bihar. During British Raj, it served as the administrative headquarters and was considered the most populous urban centre of the historical Shahabad district.

topPlaces.name: Piprahiya, Bhojpur
topPlaces.description: Piprahiya is a village in the southeastern part of Arrah block in Bhojpur district, Bihar, India. As of 2011, its population was 2,465, in 371 households.

topPlaces.name: Garaiyan, Bhojpur
topPlaces.description: Garaiyan, also spelled Gareyan or Gariyan, is a small village in Arrah block of Bhojpur district, Bihar, India. As of 2011, its population was 2, in 1 household, making it the least populous village in Bhojpur district.

topPlaces.name: Gothahula
topPlaces.description: Gothahula is a village in the southeastern part of Arrah block in Bhojpur district, Bihar, India. As of 2011, its population was 5,488, in 922 households.

items.note: Parashanatha Temple is a Jain temple dedicated to Parasnath in Masarh village near Arrah in Bihar.
Babu Veer Kunwar Singh Ramna Maidan is located in Arrah, Bhojpur District, Bihar. It is one of the largest urban parks in Arrah and is spread across 60 acres of land.
Veer Kunwar Singh Stadium is a multi-purpose stadium located in Arrah, Bihar. The stadium has capacity of 10,000 spectators.

The siege of Arrah (27 July – 3 August 1857) took place during the Indian Mutiny (also known as the Indian Rebellion of 1857). It was the eight-day defence of a fortified outbuilding, occupied by a combination of 18 civilians and 50 members of the Bengal Military Police Battalion, against 2,500 to 3,000 mutinying Bengal Native Infantry sepoys from three regiments and an estimated 8,000 men from irregular forces commanded by Kunwar Singh, the local zamindar or chieftain who controlled the Jagdishpur estate.

Arrah (also spelled as Arra, Arah, transliterated as Ara) is a city, a municipal corporation and the administrative headquarters of Bhojpur district (formerly known as Shahabad district) in the Indian state of Bihar. During British Raj, it served as the administrative headquarters and was considered the most populous urban centre of the historical Shahabad district.
Piprahiya is a village in the southeastern part of Arrah block in Bhojpur district, Bihar, India. As of 2011, its population was 2,465, in 371 households.

Garaiyan, also spelled Gareyan or Gariyan, is a small village in Arrah block of Bhojpur district, Bihar, India. As of 2011, its population was 2, in 1 household, making it the least populous village in Bhojpur district.
Gothahula is a village in the southeastern part of Arrah block in Bhojpur district, Bihar, India. As of 2011, its population was 5,488, in 922 households.

faq.q: What is the best time to visit Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah?
faq.a: The best time to visit Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah is Oct – Mar. Summers are 25–42°C and winters are 7–24°C.

faq.q: How do I reach Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah?
faq.a: The nearest airport is Jay Prakash Narayan Airport, Patna (42 km) and the nearest railway station is Patna Junction (47 km). Roads are generally good; check seasonal/monsoon conditions before high-altitude or remote stretches.

faq.q: How many days are enough for Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah?
faq.a: 3 days are usually enough to cover the top attractions of Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah at a relaxed pace.

faq.q: How much does a hotel cost in Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah?
faq.a: Stays in Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah start from ₹900 per night, with options across 4 price categories.

faq.q: What are the top places to visit in Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah?
faq.a: The top attractions are Parashanatha temple, Arrah, Ramna Maidan, Veer Kunwar Singh Stadium, Siege of Arrah, Arrah.
